figures.name: Abu Bakr
figures.significance: First Caliph; held the Muslim community together after Muhammad death

figures.name: Umar ibn al-Khattab
figures.significance: Conquered Persia, Syria, and Egypt in a decade; established Islamic governance foundations

figures.name: Khalid ibn al-Walid
figures.significance: Undefeated general; called the Sword of Allah

name: Umayyad Caliphate
note: Aggressively Arab-supremacist; non-Arab Muslims (mawali) had second-class status — this fueled the Abbasid revolution

figures.name: Muawiya I
figures.significance: Founded the Umayyad dynasty; shifted the caliphate from elective to hereditary

figures.name: Tariq ibn Ziyad
figures.significance: Conquered Visigothic Spain (711); Gibraltar is named after him (Jabal Tariq)

name: Abbasid Caliphate
note: Ruling dynasty remained Arab (Qurayshi lineage); Arabic as the language of religion and law. Persian bureaucrats (Barmakid viziers) dominated administration creating a Persianate court overlay — but the legitimacy anchor was Arab/Islamic, not Persian

figures.name: Al-Mansur
figures.significance: Built Baghdad; the city became the largest in the world

figures.name: Harun al-Rashid
figures.significance: The golden-age Caliph of the Thousand and One Nights; patron of the House of Wisdom

figures.name: Al-Muizz li-Din Allah
figures.significance: Founded Cairo; established Al-Azhar mosque and university

name: Ayyubid Sultanate
note: Saladin was Kurdish but the court operated fully in Arabic-Islamic culture

figures.name: Saladin (Salah ad-Din)
figures.significance: Recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders (1187); defining figure of Islamic resistance to the Crusades

name: Mamluk Sultanate
note: Turkic slave-soldier rulers but court and administration were Arabic-Islamic

figures.name: Baybars
figures.significance: Defeated the Mongols at Ain Jalut (1260); expelled the Crusaders
